cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to work through this problem step by step. It's a bit complex, but I'll take it slowly. First, let me restate the problem to make sure I understand it. There's a teacher with four students. The teacher picks four consecutive two-digit integers, forming set S. Each student gets one of these numbers. The teacher tells the class that the numbers are four consecutive two-digit integers, that one of them is divisible by 6, and another is divisible by 7 (different numbers). Then the teacher asks if anyone can figure out what S is. All four students say "no" in unison. But then, after hearing that all four said "no," each student can figure out S. We need to find the sum of all possible values of the greatest element of S. Alright, so the key points here are: 1. Four consecutive two-digit integers. Let's denote them as n, n+1, n+2, n+3. So n is between 10 and 97 (since n+3 must be a two-digit number, so n ≤ 97). 2. One number is divisible by 6, another by 7. These are different numbers, so there's at least one multiple of 6 and one multiple of 7 in the four numbers. 3. All four students initially say they can't determine S.
